A Snowpark Python UDF registered with the ML model handles batch scoring on arriving transactions. A Stream + Task monitors the scored transactions table and routes high-risk records to an alert staging table. An External Function calls the notification endpoint (PagerDuty, Slack, etc.) with alert details. option **A** uses Dynamic Tables for alerting, but DTs cannot trigger side effects like sending notifications. option **C** uses classic Snowpipe (higher latency) with 5-minute scheduling — too slow. option **D** uses a Dynamic Table with a Python UDF — UDFs in DTs force FULL refresh mode, adding latency and cost.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/dynamic-tables-intro

questions.stem: A data engineer configures a Snowflake external table on an S3 bucket. The `CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE` definition includes `PARTITION BY (TO_DATE(METADATA$FILENAME, 'YYYY/MM/DD'))`. What does this `PARTITION BY` clause accomplish?

options.text: It physically moves files into date-organized S3 folders
It creates a virtual partition column derived from the file path that Snowflake uses for partition pruning when queries filter by date
It restricts the external table to only read files from the specified date partition
It creates an automatic schedule to load new date partitions into an internal table

questions.explanation:
The `PARTITION BY` clause on an external table defines a virtual partition column derived from metadata (like `METADATA$FILENAME`) or other expressions. When a query filters on this column (e.g., `WHERE date_partition = '2024-01-15'`), Snowflake uses the partition pruning to scan only files matching that date prefix, dramatically reducing the data read from S3. The files are not moved (A). The table is not restricted to one date (C). No automatic loading is configured (D).

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-external-intro

questions.stem: A data engineer uses a scheduled task with `SCHEDULE = '5 MINUTE'`. In which timezone does Snowflake evaluate the 5-minute interval?

options.text: The timezone of the Snowflake account's `TIMEZONE` parameter
UTC — minute-interval schedules always use UTC regardless of session or account timezone
The timezone of the user who created the task
The timezone of the warehouse's home region

questions.explanation:
For tasks using minute-based interval schedules (`SCHEDULE = 'N MINUTE'`), Snowflake operates in UTC. The task fires every N minutes from when it was last run, measured in UTC time. For timezone-aware scheduling (e.g., 'run at 9 AM Eastern'), engineers use CRON expressions with an explicit timezone: `SCHEDULE = 'USING CRON 0 9 * * * America/New_York'`. Session timezone (C) and account timezone (A) do not affect minute-interval scheduling. Warehouse home region (D) is irrelevant to task scheduling.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/streams-intro

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to add a new column `LOADED_AT TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()` to an existing large table `ORDERS` (500 billion rows). Which statement correctly adds the column?

options.text: `ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN loaded_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()` — executes instantly as a metadata operation
`ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN loaded_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()` — triggers a full table rewrite of all 500 billion rows
`CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE orders AS SELECT *, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP() AS loaded_at FROM orders` — required for adding columns with defaults
Adding a column with DEFAULT is not supported in Snowflake; the column must be added as NULL and then backfilled

questions.explanation:
In Snowflake, `ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN` is a metadata-only operation — it executes nearly instantly regardless of table size. Snowflake's micro-partition architecture stores column metadata separately, so adding a new column (even with a DEFAULT value) does not rewrite existing micro-partitions. Existing rows will return the DEFAULT value for the new column without physically storing it.

Option **B** is wrong — no table rewrite occurs. option **C** (CTAS) would work but is unnecessarily expensive and causes downtime.

Option **D** is wrong — DEFAULT values are fully supported in `ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN`.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to pivot aggregated sales data from rows to columns, creating one column per quarter (`Q1`, `Q2`, `Q3`, `Q4`). The source has columns `(year, quarter, sales)`. Which Snowflake SQL achieves this?

options.text: `SELECT year, SUM(CASE WHEN quarter='Q1' THEN sales END) AS Q1, SUM(CASE WHEN quarter='Q2' THEN sales END) AS Q2, SUM(CASE WHEN quarter='Q3' THEN sales END) AS Q3, SUM(CASE WHEN quarter='Q4' THEN sales END) AS Q4 FROM sales_data GROUP BY year`
`SELECT * FROM sales_data PIVOT (SUM(sales) FOR quarter IN ('Q1','Q2','Q3','Q4'))`
Both A and B are valid approaches that produce equivalent results
`SELECT year, PIVOT_COLUMN(quarter, sales) FROM sales_data GROUP BY year`

questions.explanation:
Snowflake supports two approaches to pivot aggregated data: (A) Manual CASE WHEN pivoting — explicit but verbose; (B) The native `PIVOT` operator — concise and SQL-standard. Both produce equivalent results for this use case. The `PIVOT (SUM(sales) FOR quarter IN ('Q1','Q2','Q3','Q4'))` syntax is Snowflake-native and creates columns `'Q1'`, `'Q2'`, `'Q3'`, `'Q4'`. The CASE WHEN approach offers more flexibility for complex transformations. option **D** uses a non-existent `PIVOT_COLUMN()` function.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tasks-intro

questions.stem: A data engineer has an ELT pipeline that runs a MERGE statement on a 500GB clustered table. After the MERGE, the clustering quality degrades significantly (AVERAGE_OVERLAPS increases). What Snowflake feature maintains clustering quality over time without manual intervention?

options.text: Automatic Clustering (AC) — a Snowflake service that continuously reclust ers tables in the background based on their cluster key
`ALTER TABLE RECLUSTER` scheduled in a nightly task to restore clustering quality
Enable `SEARCH OPTIMIZATION` on the table to maintain micro-partition organization
Set `AUTO_VACUUM = TRUE` on the table to trigger automatic reclustering after DML

questions.explanation:
Automatic Clustering (AC) is Snowflake's fully managed background service that monitors tables with cluster keys and continuously reorganizes micro-partitions to maintain clustering quality after DML operations (INSERT, UPDATE, MERGE). It runs asynchronously and charges credits for the reclustering work. option **B** (manual `ALTER TABLE RECLUSTER` in a task) works but is manual and requires scheduling. option **C** (SEARCH OPTIMIZATION) is for point-lookup queries, not clustering maintenance. option **D** (`AUTO_VACUUM = TRUE`) is a PostgreSQL concept; Snowflake does not have this property.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions

questions.stem: A data engineer creates a task with `SCHEDULE = 'USING CRON 0 8 * * 1-5 America/Chicago'`. On a US federal holiday (Monday), the task fires at 8 AM Chicago time. The business team expected the pipeline to skip US holidays. What must the engineer add to implement business-day-only execution?

options.text: Change the CRON to `USING CRON 0 8 * * 1-5 America/Chicago SKIP_HOLIDAYS=US` to activate built-in holiday skipping
Add a `WHEN` clause that queries a holiday reference table and returns FALSE on holidays, causing the task to be SKIPPED on those days
Snowflake's task CRON scheduler automatically skips US federal holidays on weekday schedules
Replace the CRON schedule with a Dynamic Table that only refreshes on business days

questions.explanation:
Snowflake's task scheduler does not have built-in holiday awareness — CRON `1-5` means Monday through Friday by calendar day, regardless of holidays (**A** and **C** are wrong). To skip holidays, the engineer adds a `WHEN` clause: `WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM holiday_calendar WHERE holiday_date = CURRENT_DATE() AND country = 'US') = 0` — this returns FALSE on holidays, causing the task to be SKIPPED. The holiday calendar table must be maintained by the team. option **D** using Dynamic Tables is architecturally inappropriate for holiday-skipping logic.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/dynamic-tables-intro
